heroOverview: Hematosalpinx is the accumulation of blood within the lumen of one or both fallopian tubes, most commonly caused by a ruptured or leaking ectopic pregnancy, endometriosis seeding the tubal mucosa, or obstruction of the tube from a prior infection or surgical adhesion. The trapped blood distends the tube into a tender adnexal mass that can be detected on pelvic ultrasound as a sausage-shaped cystic structure with internal echoes. The clinical urgency of hematosalpinx ranges from a surgical emergency — when an ectopic pregnancy is rupturing and causing intra-abdominal haemorrhage — to a chronic, slowly progressive problem such as isolated tubal endometriosis. Treatment is directed at the underlying cause; when fertility preservation is a priority, a laparoscopic approach allows drainage, salpingostomy, or tube-sparing excision of the responsible lesion.

keyStats.prevalence: Ectopic pregnancy, the leading cause, occurs in approximately 1–2% of all pregnancies in developed countries; tubal endometriosis is found in roughly 10–20% of women with confirmed endometriosis
keyStats.demographics: Affects women of reproductive age; peak incidence of ectopic-related hematosalpinx is between ages 25–34, the peak reproductive years
keyStats.avgAge: Reproductive-aged women; endometriosis-related cases peak between ages 30–45
keyStats.globalCases: Ectopic pregnancy accounts for an estimated 1–2% of all pregnancies worldwide and is a leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality

definition: Hematosalpinx (ICD-10: N83.6) is the abnormal collection of blood within the fallopian tube, producing distension of the tubal lumen and, in many cases, a clinically detectable adnexal mass. The fallopian tube normally serves as a conduit for the ovum from the ovary to the uterus; obstruction or invasion of this structure by an ectopic pregnancy, endometriotic implants, a prior pelvic infection causing salpingitis, or any event that damages the tubal wall can result in blood being trapped and accumulating over hours to weeks. Acute hematosalpinx most commonly presents in the setting of a tubal ectopic pregnancy, where trophoblastic tissue erodes the thin muscular wall, producing haemorrhage into the tube before or after frank rupture. Chronic hematosalpinx tends to accumulate more slowly when endometriosis, post-inflammatory obstruction, or an obstructed tubal pregnancy slowly bleeds over weeks. The condition is classified within the spectrum of adnexal pathology and managed by gynecologists and reproductive endocrinologists, with emergency surgical input when rupture and peritoneal haemorrhage occur. Ultrasound, serum beta-hCG quantification, and diagnostic laparoscopy are the central investigative tools. Infertility is a significant long-term consequence because the tubal architecture is disrupted whether the blood is evacuated surgically or absorbed spontaneously.

typesClassification.description: Blood accumulates around and within a tubal ectopic pregnancy as trophoblastic tissue invades the tubal wall. This is the most clinically urgent form; it can evolve to rupture and life-threatening intra-abdominal haemorrhage within hours.

Endometriotic implants within or around the tube cause cyclical bleeding into the tubal lumen each menstrual cycle, producing a slowly accumulating haematosalpinx that presents as a persistent adnexal mass and chronic pelvic pain.

A prior episode of salpingitis or pelvic inflammatory disease leaves the tube obstructed and adherent; blood from minor tubal haemorrhage or retrograde menstruation accumulates because it cannot drain, producing a hydrosalpinx-like mass containing old blood.

Intra-operative or post-operative tubal haemorrhage following uterine surgery, hysteroscopy, or laparoscopy can fill a ligated or obstructed tube with blood, typically discovered incidentally on follow-up imaging.

primarySymptoms: Acute pelvic or lower abdominal pain on one or both sides, typically sharp or cramping in character, arising from tubal distension by the accumulating blood and from any associated peritoneal irritation.
Amenorrhea or a missed period with a positive pregnancy test, which in the context of pelvic pain strongly suggests an ectopic pregnancy causing hematosalpinx and warrants urgent evaluation.
Vaginal bleeding or spotting that may be dark or brownish, reflecting small amounts of blood passing from the tube through the uterine cavity — a classic but unreliable sign of ectopic pregnancy.
An adnexal mass palpated on bimanual examination or detected on imaging — a tender, discrete, sausage-shaped or rounded fullness lateral and posterior to the uterus.
Shoulder-tip pain on inspiration, caused by sub-diaphragmatic irritation from blood that has leaked into the peritoneal cavity from a perforating or leaking tube.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying the acute pain, a non-specific but common accompaniment to any acute pelvic haemorrhagic event.
Chronic intermittent pelvic pain, particularly cyclically timed with menstruation, in women with endometriosis-related hematosalpinx, reflecting repeated monthly episodes of tubal bleeding.
Deep dyspareunia — pain during intercourse felt deep in the pelvis — associated with endometriosis-related hematosalpinx and peritubal adhesions that restrict normal uterine movement.
Dizziness, light-headedness, and haemodynamic instability — pallor, rapid pulse, low blood pressure — when a ruptured ectopic pregnancy causes significant intra-abdominal haemorrhage.
Infertility or difficulty conceiving, which may be the presenting complaint when a chronic hematosalpinx is identified incidentally on transvaginal ultrasound during an infertility evaluation.
earlyWarningSigns: A positive pregnancy test accompanied by pelvic pain or minimal spotting without a confirmed intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound.
A slowly enlarging adnexal mass identified on serial ultrasound that contains internal echoes consistent with blood products.
Cyclical pelvic pain in a woman with known or suspected endometriosis, suggesting progressive tubal involvement.
Serum beta-hCG levels that rise more slowly than expected for a normally located pregnancy, prompting urgent re-evaluation.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe lower abdominal pain with haemodynamic instability — rapid pulse, low blood pressure, pale clammy skin — indicating tubal rupture with significant intra-abdominal haemorrhage; this is a surgical emergency requiring immediate intervention.
Shoulder-tip pain with abdominal rigidity or rebound tenderness, confirming peritoneal blood irritating the diaphragm.
Syncopal episode or near-syncope in a woman of reproductive age with lower abdominal pain and a positive pregnancy test.
Any positive pregnancy test with worsening pelvic pain and no confirmed intrauterine sac on ultrasound should prompt same-day emergency gynaecological assessment.

causes.description: A fertilised egg implants in the fallopian tube rather than the uterine cavity, most often in the ampullary region. Trophoblastic tissue invades the thin tubal muscularis, eroding blood vessels and generating haemorrhage into the tubal lumen. Absent treatment, progressive blood accumulation leads to tubal rupture in approximately 15–20% of cases within the first 6–8 weeks.

Endometriotic glands and stroma implant within the tubal mucosa or peritubally; each menstrual cycle activates these implants, causing cyclical tubal bleeding that accumulates in the obstructed lumen. Tubal endometriosis is present in 10–20% of women with confirmed endometriosis and produces a steadily enlarging blood-filled tube over months to years.

causes.cause: Prior salpingitis and pelvic inflammatory disease
causes.description: Gonorrheal or chlamydial salpingitis leaves the tube scarred and distal obstruction is common. Minor retrograde bleeding or haemorrhage from inflamed tubal mucosa fills the obstructed tube; the resulting haematosalpinx may persist long after the acute infection resolves. A prior episode of PID is present in the history of approximately 40–60% of women presenting with chronic tubo-ovarian adnexal masses.

Twisting of the tube on its mesenteric pedicle compromises venous return before arterial flow, producing haemorrhagic infarction and blood accumulation within the lumen. Tubal torsion, though uncommon, typically presents with severe acute unilateral pain and may occur independently of ovarian torsion, particularly in tubes that are elongated or contain a cyst.

causes.cause: Uterine or pelvic surgery with tubal haemorrhage
causes.description: Hysteroscopic procedures, myomectomy, hysterectomy, and laparoscopic surgeries can occasionally produce tubal haemorrhage from instrumentation of the cornual region or from post-operative adhesion formation that obstructs tubal drainage, resulting in blood accumulation discovered on follow-up imaging.

riskFactors.description: A history of one ectopic pregnancy increases the risk of a subsequent ectopic by approximately 10-fold; tubal scarring from the first event reduces ciliary function and creates a structural environment for repeat implantation and hematosalpinx.

Each episode of PID from gonorrhoea or chlamydia increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy 6–10-fold by impairing tubal ciliary function and producing intraluminal adhesions; prompt treatment of STIs dramatically reduces this risk.
Moderate-to-severe endometriosis involving the fallopian tubes directly produces hematosalpinx through cyclical tubal implant bleeding; women with Stage III–IV endometriosis are at highest risk.

riskFactors.factor: Intrauterine device use at time of STI acquisition
riskFactors.description: An IUD does not cause infection but, if a sexually transmitted infection is acquired while an IUD is present, ascending infection and salpingitis are more likely; regular STI screening removes this risk.

In vitro fertilisation (IVF) involves ovarian hyperstimulation and transvaginal oocyte retrieval, which can rarely cause tubal haemorrhage as a procedural complication; hydrosalpinx from prior tubal damage also directly impairs IVF success rates.

Cigarette smoking impairs tubal ciliary motility and increases the risk of tubal ectopic implantation; smokers have approximately double the ectopic pregnancy rate of non-smokers, a directly modifiable risk.

Any prior pelvic surgery can produce peritoneal adhesions that distort tubal anatomy, impair ovum transport, and create conditions for retained blood; appendicectomy, bowel surgery, and prior caesarean section are all associated with elevated ectopic risk.

affectedDemographics: Reproductive-aged women, particularly those aged 25–39, in whom ectopic pregnancy and endometriosis are most prevalent
Women with a prior history of pelvic inflammatory disease, STI, or prior ectopic pregnancy
Women with confirmed or suspected endometriosis who have cyclical pelvic pain and adnexal masses
Women undergoing infertility evaluation in whom chronic hematosalpinx is an incidental finding on transvaginal ultrasound
Perimenopausal women in whom slowly progressive endometriosis-related tubal disease may produce a new adnexal mass
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of hematosalpinx begins with the clinical context: an acute presentation in a woman with a positive pregnancy test and pelvic pain is an ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise, requiring urgent serum beta-hCG quantification and immediate transvaginal ultrasound. Transvaginal ultrasound is the primary imaging modality: it characteristically shows a sausage-shaped or tubular adnexal mass separate from the ovary, containing homogeneous or heterogeneous internal echoes consistent with blood products. A positive pregnancy test combined with no visible intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound and a serum beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone (typically 1,500–2,000 IU/L in most centres) confirms ectopic pregnancy with high specificity. Serial beta-hCG measurements assess the trajectory: levels doubling appropriately every 48 hours suggest a viable intrauterine pregnancy; rising but insufficiently rapid or plateauing levels point toward an ectopic location. When the aetiology is non-ectopic, MRI of the pelvis characterises the mass further, distinguishing haematosalpinx from hydrosalpinx, endometrioma, or other adnexal pathology based on the signal characteristics of blood products at different stages of haemoglobin breakdown. Definitive diagnosis and simultaneous treatment in most cases are achieved at diagnostic laparoscopy, which provides direct visualisation of the tube, the nature of any haemorrhage, the extent of endometriosis, and the state of the contralateral tube and ovary.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes pregnancy and, when combined with imaging, helps localise it; serial measurements assess pregnancy viability and trajectory in suspected ectopic cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from a vein in the arm; results are usually available within 1–2 hours. A second measurement 48 hours later is often needed to track the rate of rise.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to identify the adnexal mass, confirm or exclude an intrauterine gestational sac, and detect free pelvic fluid consistent with haemorrhage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A slim ultrasound probe is gently inserted into the vagina; the examination takes 10–15 minutes and causes mild discomfort. It provides significantly better image resolution than abdominal ultrasound for adnexal structures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the content of the adnexal mass (blood products at different ages produce distinct MRI signal patterns), and differentiates hematosalpinx from endometrioma, hydrosalpinx, or tubo-ovarian abscess when ultrasound is inconclusive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30–45 minutes; no radiation is involved. A contrast agent is not routinely required for adnexal mass characterisation in most cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A single serum progesterone can help distinguish a viable intrauterine pregnancy from an ectopic or failing pregnancy; very low levels support an abnormal or ectopic location.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw alongside the beta-hCG; results take 1–4 hours in most centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides direct visualisation of the fallopian tubes, peritoneal cavity, and pelvic organs; simultaneously allows definitive surgical treatment — salpingostomy, salpingectomy, or endometriosis ablation — at the same sitting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A keyhole surgical procedure under general anaesthesia. Three small incisions (each less than 1 cm) are made in the abdomen. You recover for 1–4 hours and are usually discharged the same day or the next morning.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses haemoglobin level and clotting function in cases of suspected significant tubal haemorrhage, guiding transfusion decisions and surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood sample; results are available within 30–60 minutes in an emergency setting.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is urgently driven by the underlying cause and the haemodynamic status of the patient. An ectopic pregnancy with evidence of tubal rupture, significant haemoperitoneum (pooling of blood in the pelvis), or haemodynamic compromise is a surgical emergency requiring immediate operative laparoscopy or laparotomy. In haemodynamically stable women with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy who have no contraindications, medical management with a single or double dose of intramuscular methotrexate — a folate antagonist that halts trophoblastic cell division — achieves resolution without surgery in approximately 85–90% of carefully selected cases. Eligibility for methotrexate requires an unruptured ectopic, no cardiac activity visible on ultrasound in the ectopic sac, serum beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L (some centres use 3,000 IU/L), and absence of liver or renal disease. Serial beta-hCG monitoring after methotrexate confirms declining levels and successful resolution. Surgical options for stable ectopic pregnancy include laparoscopic salpingostomy — incising the tube to remove the ectopic contents while preserving the tube — and salpingectomy, which removes the affected tube. Salpingostomy offers a theoretical fertility advantage but persistent trophoblast complicates approximately 8–15% of cases and requires surveillance or further treatment. For hematosalpinx caused by endometriosis, laparoscopic excision of the endometriotic lesion, drainage of the tube, and medical hormonal suppression (GnRH agonists, progestins, or combined oral contraceptives) to prevent recurrence are the standard approach. Post-infective or obstructive hematosalpinx may be managed expectantly if small and asymptomatic, or by laparoscopic drainage and adhesiolysis when causing pain or infertility.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (intramuscular)
medicalTreatments.description: The primary non-surgical treatment for haemodynamically stable unruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy. Methotrexate inhibits DNA synthesis in rapidly dividing trophoblastic cells, halting ectopic growth and allowing the body to resorb the pregnancy. Given as a single IM dose (50 mg/m² body surface area) or in a two-dose protocol; serial beta-hCG monitoring at days 4, 7, and weekly thereafter confirms response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves complete resolution without surgery in approximately 85–90% of carefully selected patients with initial beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L.

medicalTreatments.name: GnRH agonists for endometriosis-related hematosalpinx
medicalTreatments.description: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (leuprolide, goserelin, nafarelin) suppress ovarian oestrogen production, inducing a pseudo-menopausal state that causes endometriotic implants to regress and stops cyclical tubal bleeding. Used for 3–6 months after surgical excision to prevent recurrence, with add-back oestrogen to manage menopausal side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces endometriosis pain scores by approximately 80% and recurrence rates by 40–50% at 12 months compared with surgery alone; symptom recurrence after cessation is common.

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP)
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous or cyclic combined hormonal contraception suppresses ovulation and reduces the monthly activation of endometriotic implants, limiting cyclical tubal bleeding. It is used as long-term secondary prevention after surgical management of endometriosis-related hematosalpinx.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces endometriosis symptom recurrence by approximately 40–60% in women maintained on continuous OCP after surgical treatment compared with expectant management.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and supportive care
medicalTreatments.description: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) and paracetamol manage acute and chronic pelvic pain associated with hematosalpinx. NSAIDs are additionally useful for their prostaglandin-inhibiting effect in endometriosis-related pain. Opioids are reserved for severe acute pain while awaiting definitive diagnosis or treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NSAIDs reduce endometriosis-associated pain in approximately 70% of users; they do not treat the underlying structural cause and are a temporising measure pending definitive management.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic salpingostomy
surgicalOptions.description: A small incision is made along the antimesenteric border of the distended tube; the ectopic or obstructing contents are removed with irrigation and the tube is left to heal spontaneously without suturing (linear salpingostomy). This tube-sparing approach is preferred when the contralateral tube is absent or damaged and fertility preservation is the priority.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Intrauterine pregnancy rate of approximately 65–70% within 18 months in women with the contralateral tube damaged; persistent trophoblast requiring further treatment in 8–15% of ectopic cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic salpingectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Complete removal of the affected fallopian tube. Preferred over salpingostomy when the contralateral tube is healthy, when tubal rupture has occurred, or when the tube is extensively damaged. It eliminates the risk of persistent trophoblast and provides a definitive tissue diagnosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Eliminates recurrent ectopic risk in the removed tube entirely; subsequent intrauterine pregnancy rates in women with a normal contralateral tube are approximately 65–80% within two years.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgery for haemodynamically unstable patients with significant haemoperitoneum from a ruptured ectopic pregnancy. Allows rapid haemostasis, salpingectomy, and blood salvage; replaced by laparoscopy in all but the most severely compromised patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Life-saving in the setting of ruptured ectopic haemorrhage; perioperative mortality in resourced settings is less than 0.1% with timely intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Expectant management for small stable ectopic pregnancies
alternativeTreatments.description: In selected women with declining serum beta-hCG levels below 1,000 IU/L, no cardiac activity, and minimal symptoms, watchful waiting with serial beta-hCG monitoring every 48–72 hours can be appropriate; approximately 50–70% of very-low-hCG ectopic pregnancies resolve spontaneously.

alternativeTreatments.name: Laparoscopic adhesiolysis for post-infective hematosalpinx
alternativeTreatments.description: Division of peritubal adhesions from prior PID releases the tube from distorting scar tissue, improves tubal mobility, and may restore function. Outcomes depend heavily on the severity of mucosal damage within the tube.

whySeeSpecialist: Any woman with a positive pregnancy test and pelvic pain must be evaluated urgently by a gynecologist — a ruptured ectopic pregnancy can be fatal within hours of symptom onset. A gynecologist or reproductive endocrinologist should also assess women in whom hematosalpinx is discovered on imaging without a current pregnancy, particularly if fertility is desired, since the underlying cause (endometriosis, prior PID, tubal obstruction) has direct implications for future conception. Delayed diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy remains a leading cause of preventable first-trimester maternal death.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a gynecologist with laparoscopic surgical skills and access to emergency operating facilities for ectopic pregnancy. For non-ectopic hematosalpinx, a reproductive endocrinologist or specialist with expertise in endometriosis surgery is ideal, particularly if fertility preservation matters. Confirm the centre has 24-hour transvaginal ultrasound and emergency surgery capability for ectopic presentations. Ask about the surgeon's annual ectopic surgery volume and their approach to tube-sparing versus salpingectomy based on individual circumstances.
preventionStrategies: Screen for and promptly treat sexually transmitted infections (chlamydia, gonorrhoea) to prevent salpingitis and tubal scarring.
Use barrier contraception to reduce STI acquisition risk.
Undergo regular pelvic surveillance if diagnosed with endometriosis to detect tubal involvement early.
Attend early ultrasound assessment at 6–7 weeks in any pregnancy following prior ectopic pregnancy to confirm intrauterine implantation.
Stop smoking to halve the risk of ectopic implantation.
Seek prompt assessment of any pelvic pain with a positive pregnancy test rather than waiting for pain to worsen.
lifestyleModifications: Report any pelvic pain with a positive pregnancy test immediately rather than managing it at home with analgesics.
Keep a menstrual diary if you have endometriosis-related pelvic pain, noting the relationship of pain to your cycle, to help clinicians track disease progression.
Attend all follow-up appointments after methotrexate treatment, since beta-hCG monitoring at days 4, 7, and weekly is essential to confirm resolution.
After a conservative laparoscopic salpingostomy, attend serial ultrasound follow-up until the ectopic site is confirmed resolved.
Discuss the optimal contraception strategy with your clinician after an ectopic event; IUDs and progesterone-only methods do not increase ectopic risk in women without prior tubal disease.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods including oily fish (omega-3 fatty acids), colourful vegetables, and olive oil to support reduction of endometriosis-related inflammation
High-fibre foods to maintain bowel regularity — constipation aggravates pelvic pain in women with endometriosis
Folate-rich foods (leafy greens, lentils, fortified cereals) during reproductive years to support future pregnancy health
avoid: Excess red and processed meat, associated with higher endometriosis risk in some observational studies
Excessive alcohol consumption, which may exacerbate inflammatory pelvic pain
Folate-antagonising foods and supplements during or shortly after methotrexate treatment — alcohol, multivitamins containing folate, and NSAIDs should be avoided while beta-hCG is declining

exerciseGuidelines: During an acute hematosalpinx event, rest and avoidance of strenuous physical activity are advised until the underlying cause is treated and haemodynamic stability is confirmed. After recovery from laparoscopic surgery, light activity resumes at 1–2 weeks and most women return to normal activity within 2–4 weeks. Women with chronic endometriosis-related hematosalpinx benefit from low-impact aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, yoga), which reduces endometriosis pain scores by approximately 50% in observational studies. High-impact activity should be approached cautiously during periods of significant pelvic pain.
dailyManagement: After methotrexate treatment, attend every scheduled beta-hCG test — missing a test is not safe, as rising levels signal treatment failure.
Note any worsening pain, bleeding, or shoulder-tip pain after treatment and seek emergency assessment immediately.
After surgery, complete the full course of prescribed analgesics and antibiotics and attend all wound-check appointments.
Keep a symptom diary if endometriosis is driving recurrent hematosalpinx; document pain severity and timing relative to menstruation.
Discuss future fertility plans proactively with a reproductive endocrinologist after any episode of hematosalpinx that affects tubal integrity.
prognosis: The prognosis of hematosalpinx depends entirely on the underlying cause and the timeliness of treatment. Ectopic pregnancy treated promptly with methotrexate or laparoscopic surgery has an excellent short-term outcome: first-trimester maternal mortality from ectopic pregnancy in settings with access to emergency care is below 0.5 per 1,000 cases, but delayed diagnosis in resource-limited settings carries significantly higher risk. Future fertility after a single treated ectopic pregnancy is reasonable: intrauterine pregnancy rates of 60–70% within two years are reported, though the risk of another ectopic increases. Chronic hematosalpinx from endometriosis has a variable prognosis depending on disease stage; surgical excision followed by hormonal suppression achieves pain relief in approximately 70–80% of women, but disease recurrence is common at a rate of approximately 20–25% per year after stopping suppressive therapy. Tubal damage from any cause reduces ipsilateral tubal function permanently; IVF may ultimately be required for conception if both tubes are affected.
recoveryTimeline: After laparoscopic salpingostomy or salpingectomy, most women are discharged within 24 hours. Shoulder-tip pain from residual gas subsides within 24–48 hours. Incision site discomfort resolves over 5–10 days. Normal activities including light work resume at 1–2 weeks. Heavy exercise and sexual intercourse can usually resume at 2–4 weeks. After methotrexate treatment, full resolution — confirmed by undetectable beta-hCG — typically takes 2–8 weeks with weekly monitoring. Women attempting another pregnancy are advised to wait at least 3 months after methotrexate for folate levels to normalise.
complications: Tubal rupture and haemoperitoneum — the most immediately life-threatening complication of ectopic-related hematosalpinx, requiring emergency surgery to achieve haemostasis.
Infertility — tubal scarring and loss of mucosal architecture from any cause of hematosalpinx permanently impairs ipsilateral tubal function; bilateral involvement necessitates IVF.
Persistent trophoblast after salpingostomy — residual trophoblastic tissue continues to grow and bleed after conservative surgery in 8–15% of cases, requiring methotrexate or repeat surgery.
Recurrent ectopic pregnancy — the ipsilateral tube is at approximately 10-fold elevated risk of a subsequent ectopic after the first event.
Endometriosis progression — untreated or inadequately suppressed tubal endometriosis continues to accumulate blood, enlarge the hematosalpinx, and cause progressive peritoneal adhesion formation.
Tubo-ovarian abscess — secondary infection of a chronic hematosalpinx can produce an acutely inflamed tubo-ovarian complex requiring emergency drainage and intravenous antibiotics.

supportResources.name: Ectopic Pregnancy Trust — Patient support and information
supportResources.description: UK-based specialist patient organisation providing detailed information, lived-experience support, and clinical guidance on ectopic pregnancy and hematosalpinx.

supportResources.name: NHS — Ectopic pregnancy: overview
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guide to recognising, diagnosing, and treating ectopic pregnancy including emergency warning signs.

supportResources.name: Endometriosis UK — Patient resources
supportResources.description: Information and peer support for women with endometriosis, including tubal endometriosis as a cause of hematosalpinx.

sources.title: ACOG Practice Bulletin 191 — Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy, 2018
Barnhart KT et al. Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy. JAMA, 2009
Hajenius PJ et al. Interventions for tubal ectopic pregnancy. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2007
NICE guideline NG126 — Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management, 2019
ACOG Practice Bulletin 114 — Management of Endometriosis, 2010 (reaffirmed 2018)
Mol F et al. Salpingotomy versus salpingectomy in women with tubal pregnancy. Lancet, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Endometriosis of fallopian tube
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tubal endometriosis is a cause of hematosalpinx rather than a separate diagnosis; it is distinguished by laparoscopic biopsy confirming endometriotic glands and stroma within or around the tube, not merely blood-filled tubal distension.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ovarian torsion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ovarian torsion causes acute unilateral pelvic pain with an enlarged ovary on imaging; hematosalpinx presents as a tubular adnexal mass and is distinguished by the elongated sausage-shaped ultrasound appearance and, crucially, by beta-hCG measurement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic inflammatory disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute PID causes fever, bilateral adnexal tenderness, and cervical motion tenderness with a negative pregnancy test; hematosalpinx in an ectopic pregnancy presents with a positive beta-hCG and a unilateral adnexal mass.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ovarian cyst
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An ovarian cyst is an adnexal mass arising from the ovary itself; hematosalpinx is tubular and arises from the fallopian tube, separate from the ovary, and is characterised by the elongated sausage appearance on ultrasound rather than the spherical ovarian cyst morphology.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis of fallopian tube
relatedConditions.relevance: Tubal endometriosis is the second most common cause of hematosalpinx after ectopic pregnancy, and its recognition and surgical excision are essential to prevent chronic progressive tubal blood accumulation.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic inflammatory disease
relatedConditions.relevance: PID is a major preventable risk factor for hematosalpinx: each episode of salpingitis increases the risk of tubal ectopic implantation 6–10-fold and can itself produce chronic obstructive hematosalpinx.

relatedConditions.name: Hematometra
relatedConditions.relevance: When outflow obstruction of the uterus causes blood to collect in the uterine cavity, retrograde passage of blood through the tubes can produce concurrent hematosalpinx; the two conditions may coexist in women with cervical stenosis or endometrial adhesions.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Systemic endometriosis predisposes specifically to tubal involvement; women diagnosed with endometriosis at any site should be evaluated for tubal involvement, particularly if infertility or an adnexal mass develops.

faqs.question: What is hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: Hematosalpinx is a condition in which blood accumulates inside a fallopian tube, distending it into a tender adnexal mass. It is most commonly caused by a tubal ectopic pregnancy or endometriosis, but can also result from prior pelvic infection, tubal torsion, or surgery.

faqs.question: Is hematosalpinx an emergency?
faqs.answer: It can be. When hematosalpinx is caused by an ectopic pregnancy that is rupturing, it is a surgical emergency — the fallopian tube can tear open, causing life-threatening intra-abdominal haemorrhage within hours. Any woman with a positive pregnancy test, pelvic pain, and no confirmed intrauterine sac on ultrasound should be assessed urgently in a gynaecology unit.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are acute or chronic one-sided pelvic pain, a palpable adnexal mass, vaginal spotting or bleeding, and, in ectopic pregnancy, a missed period with a positive pregnancy test. Shoulder-tip pain, dizziness, and haemodynamic instability signal significant tubal haemorrhage and require immediate emergency assessment.

faqs.question: What causes hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is a tubal ectopic pregnancy, in which a fertilised egg implants in the fallopian tube. Other causes include endometriosis of the tube, prior salpingitis from gonorrhoea or chlamydia, tubal torsion, and occasionally surgical complications. Each cause produces blood accumulation inside the tube by a different mechanism.

faqs.question: How is hematosalpinx diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines serum beta-hCG measurement with transvaginal ultrasound. The ultrasound shows a sausage-shaped tubal mass with internal echoes; a positive beta-hCG confirms pregnancy-related aetiology. Pelvic MRI characterises non-pregnancy cases, and diagnostic laparoscopy provides definitive visualisation and simultaneous treatment.

faqs.question: Can hematosalpinx be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, in selected cases. A haemodynamically stable woman with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy, no cardiac activity in the ectopic sac, and a serum beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L can be treated with methotrexate injection, which halts trophoblastic growth. Success rates reach 85–90% in appropriately selected patients, with resolution confirmed by serial beta-hCG monitoring.

faqs.question: Does hematosalpinx affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Blood-filled tube causes permanent architectural damage to the delicate tubal mucosa and ciliary cells regardless of whether the blood is surgically removed or absorbed. If both tubes are affected, natural conception is unlikely and IVF is typically required. A single affected tube with a healthy contralateral tube leaves intrauterine pregnancy rates of 60–70% within two years.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hematosalpinx and hydrosalpinx?
faqs.answer: Hydrosalpinx is a fallopian tube filled with clear serous fluid from prior infection and scarring; hematosalpinx contains blood. Both appear on ultrasound as tubular adnexal masses, but hematosalpinx shows internal echoes from blood products while hydrosalpinx is typically anechoic. Both impair fertility, but hematosalpinx has more urgent management implications when associated with an ectopic pregnancy.

faqs.question: What happens during laparoscopic salpingostomy?
faqs.answer: The surgeon makes a small incision along the fallopian tube over the blood-filled area, gently removes the contents with irrigation, and leaves the tube to heal without stitching. This tube-sparing approach is preferred when fertility preservation is a priority and the other tube is absent or damaged. Persistent trophoblast requiring additional treatment occurs in about 10% of cases.

faqs.question: Should the fallopian tube be removed for hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: That depends on the cause and the state of the other tube. Salpingectomy (tube removal) is preferred for a ruptured or extensively damaged tube, when the other tube is healthy, or when persistent trophoblast is a risk. Salpingostomy (tube-sparing surgery) is chosen when preserving that tube is essential for fertility. Your surgeon will discuss the best approach based on your individual circumstances.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after hematosalpinx surgery?
faqs.answer: After laparoscopic salpingostomy or salpingectomy, most women are discharged within 24 hours. Shoulder-tip gas pain resolves in 1–2 days. Light activity resumes at 1–2 weeks and most women return to normal life within 2–4 weeks. Sexual intercourse can usually resume at 2–4 weeks. After methotrexate treatment, resolution takes 2–8 weeks of serial monitoring.

faqs.question: Can I get pregnant after hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: Many women do conceive after a single episode of hematosalpinx treated promptly. Intrauterine pregnancy rates within two years are approximately 60–70% when one tube is removed but the other is healthy. The risk of another ectopic in the remaining tube is elevated, so early ultrasound confirmation of intrauterine implantation is essential in any subsequent pregnancy.

faqs.question: Is endometriosis a cause of hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: Yes. Endometriotic implants within or around the fallopian tube bleed each menstrual cycle, filling the tube with blood that cannot drain if the distal tube is obstructed. This produces a slowly enlarging chronic hematosalpinx that is a significant cause of tubal infertility and chronic pelvic pain in women with endometriosis.

faqs.question: What is the methotrexate treatment for hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: Methotrexate is an injection given into the muscle that stops trophoblastic cell division in an ectopic pregnancy. It is used for unruptured ectopic pregnancies in stable women with no cardiac activity on ultrasound and beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L. Serial blood tests confirm resolution over 2–8 weeks. Alcohol, folic acid supplements, and NSAIDs should be avoided during treatment.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of tubal rupture?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include sudden severe one-sided pelvic pain, shoulder-tip pain on breathing, dizziness or fainting, a racing heartbeat, and low blood pressure. These signs in a woman with a positive pregnancy test indicate a medical emergency. Call emergency services or go to the nearest emergency department immediately.

faqs.question: Can hematosalpinx resolve on its own?
faqs.answer: A very-low-beta-hCG ectopic pregnancy (below approximately 1,000 IU/L with no cardiac activity) can resolve spontaneously in 50–70% of cases under close monitoring. Most hematosalpinx cases, however, require either medical or surgical treatment, and watchful waiting is only appropriate in carefully selected, closely monitored women under specialist care.

faqs.question: How does prior pelvic infection cause hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: A prior episode of gonorrhoeal or chlamydial salpingitis scars the inside of the fallopian tube and seals the distal end. When minor tubal haemorrhage occurs — from retrograde menstruation or small vessel erosion — blood cannot exit through the blocked end and accumulates in the tube over time, producing a chronic hematosalpinx.

faqs.question: Does hematosalpinx show on ultrasound?
faqs.answer: Yes. On transvaginal ultrasound, hematosalpinx appears as a sausage-shaped or tubular adnexal structure separate from the ovary, containing low-level internal echoes that represent blood products. This finding combined with a positive pregnancy test is highly suspicious for an ectopic pregnancy and requires urgent clinical assessment.

faqs.question: Can hematosalpinx come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence depends on the cause. After salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy, the affected tube is removed and cannot re-fill. After salpingostomy, persistent or recurrent ectopic in the preserved tube occurs in approximately 10–15% of cases. Endometriosis-related hematosalpinx recurs in about 20–25% of women per year after stopping hormonal suppression.

faqs.question: What specialist treats hematosalpinx?
faqs.answer: A gynecologist manages acute presentations, particularly ectopic pregnancy. For endometriosis-related or infertility-associated hematosalpinx, a reproductive endocrinologist or endometriosis specialist is most appropriate. Emergency cases with haemodynamic instability are managed by the emergency gynaecology or general surgery team depending on local resources.

metaDescription: Hematosalpinx is blood trapped in the fallopian tube from ectopic pregnancy or endometriosis. Learn urgent symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.
